---
title: "Day 3 â€” Concepts"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

Companion to the day-3 slides. Day 3 is where Rust starts to feel like Rust: structs and enums model biological data faithfully; `Option`, `Result`, and iterators replace exception handling and "loops with state" with composable, type-checked alternatives.

## Structs

```rust
struct GenomicInterval {
    chrom: String,
    start: u64,
    end: u64,
}

let iv = GenomicInterval {
    chrom: "chr1".to_string(),
    start: 1000,
    end: 2000,
};
println!("{}-{}", iv.start, iv.end);
```

Field-by-field construction. Field privacy, derives, and tuple structs all in the â†’ [Book chapter on structs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch05-00-structs.html).

## Enums â€” sums of variants

```rust
enum Strand {
    Plus,
    Minus,
}

enum Variant {
    Snp { pos: u64, alt: u8 },
    Insertion { pos: u64, bases: Vec<u8> },
    Deletion { pos: u64, len: u64 },
}
```

Two flavours:

- **C-style** (`Strand`): just tagged variants with no data. Compiles to a single small integer.
- **Sum type** (`Variant`): each variant carries its own data. The enum is large enough to hold any of its variants, plus a tag.

The compiler insists you match every variant â€” see "exhaustive matching" below.

â†’ [Book: Defining an Enum](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch06-01-defining-an-enum.html)

## Methods â€” `impl` blocks

```rust
impl GenomicInterval {
    fn new(chrom: String, start: u64, end: u64) -> Self {
        Self { chrom, start, end }
    }

    fn length(&self) -> u64 {                // borrow self read-only
        self.end - self.start
    }

    fn shift(&mut self, by: u64) {           // borrow self mutably
        self.start += by;
        self.end   += by;
    }
}
```

First parameter is `&self`, `&mut self`, or `self` (consuming). Methods are called with `iv.length()`. Associated functions (no `self`) like `new` are called with `GenomicInterval::new(...)`.

â†’ [Book: Method Syntax](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch05-03-method-syntax.html)

## `#[derive(...)]`

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
enum Strand { Plus, Minus }
```

The compiler writes trait implementations for you. The ones you will use this week:

- **`Debug`** â€” enables `{:?}` formatting. Almost always.
- **`Clone`** â€” `.clone()` deep-copies. Almost always.
- **`Copy`** â€” assignment copies, no move. Only for types whose entire contents live on the stack (no `String`, no `Vec`).
- **`PartialEq`** â€” `==` and `!=`. Add `Eq` if equality is reflexive (most types â€” but `f64` is famously not).

## `Option<T>` â€” values that may be absent

```rust
enum Option<T> {
    Some(T),
    None,
}

fn first_g(seq: &[u8]) -> Option<usize> {
    for (i, &b) in seq.iter().enumerate() {
        if b == b'G' { return Some(i); }
    }
    None
}
```

`Option<T>` replaces `null`. The compiler will not let you use the inner value without first checking which variant you have:

```rust
match opt {
    Some(x) => use_it(x),
    None => default(),
}

if let Some(x) = opt {           // shorthand when you only care about Some
    use_it(x);
}

let x = opt.unwrap_or(0);         // value, or a default
let x = opt.expect("must be Some by here");   // panic with message if None
```

â†’ [`Option` docs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/option/enum.Option.html)

## `Result<T, E>` â€” operations that can fail

```rust
enum Result<T, E> {
    Ok(T),
    Err(E),
}
```

`Result` is `Option`'s richer sibling: when something fails, the error gets its own value of type `E`. Use it for recoverable failures (bad input, missing files, parse errors).

## The `?` operator

`?` means *"if this is `Err`, return it from the enclosing function; otherwise unwrap and continue"*:

```rust
fn parse_region(s: &str) -> Result<(String, u64, u64), ParseError> {
    let (chrom, rest) = s.split_once(':').ok_or(ParseError::MissingColon)?;
    let (start_s, end_s) = rest.split_once('-').ok_or(ParseError::MissingDash)?;
    let start: u64 = start_s.parse().map_err(|_| ParseError::InvalidStart)?;
    let end:   u64 = end_s  .parse().map_err(|_| ParseError::InvalidEnd)?;
    Ok((chrom.to_string(), start, end))
}
```

The enclosing function must itself return `Result` (or `Option`) for `?` to be valid.

â†’ [Book: ? Operator](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch09-02-recoverable-errors-with-result.html#a-shortcut-for-propagating-errors-the--operator)

## Exhaustive matching

The compiler refuses to compile a `match` that does not cover every possible input value. The wildcard `_` matches anything you have not listed:

```rust
match codon {
    b"ATG" => Some(b'M'),
    b"TAA" | b"TAG" | b"TGA" => Some(b'*'),
    _ => None,
}
```

This same mechanism catches *"I added a new enum variant and forgot to update X"* bugs at compile time.

## Iterators

Iterators are **lazy**. They produce values one at a time, only when asked. Adaptors (`map`, `filter`, `take`) build a chain; **consumers** (`collect`, `count`, `sum`, `fold`, `for`) drive it:

```rust
let gc_count = seq.iter()
    .filter(|&&b| matches!(b, b'G' | b'C'))
    .count();

let upper: Vec<u8> = seq.iter()
    .map(|&b| b.to_ascii_uppercase())
    .collect();

let mean_gc: f64 =
    sequences.iter().map(|s| gc_content(s)).sum::<f64>() / sequences.len() as f64;
```

Iterator chains compile to the same machine code as a hand-written loop (zero-cost). Use them whenever the body is *"for each item, do X, produce a value"*.

â†’ [Book: Iterators](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch13-02-iterators.html)

## Useful slice and string methods

```rust
seq.windows(3)            // every consecutive 3-byte window
seq.chunks(8)             // every 8-byte chunk (last may be shorter)
seq.iter().rev()          // reverse iteration
seq.iter().enumerate()    // (index, value) pairs

s.split_once(':')         // returns Option<(&str, &str)>
s.lines()                 // iterator over lines
s.starts_with('>')        // bool
s.parse::<u64>()          // returns Result<u64, _>
```

## Closures

```rust
let add5 = |x: i32| x + 5;
let squares: Vec<i32> = (0..10).map(|i| i * i).collect();
```

Closures borrow or move values from the surrounding scope. The three closure traits â€” `Fn`, `FnMut`, `FnOnce` â€” describe *how* they relate to their captures. For day 3 you can ignore the distinction; iterator combinators accept any of them.

â†’ [Book: Closures](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch13-01-closures.html)

## `HashMap` and the entry API

```rust
use std::collections::HashMap;

let mut counts: HashMap<Vec<u8>, usize> = HashMap::new();
for window in seq.windows(k) {
    *counts.entry(window.to_vec()).or_insert(0) += 1;
}
```

[`entry(key).or_insert(default)`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/collections/struct.HashMap.html#method.entry) returns a `&mut V` to either the existing value or a freshly-inserted default. Dereference and mutate in place. This is the canonical histogram-building pattern.

â†’ [`HashMap` docs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/collections/struct.HashMap.html)

## Recursive types

```rust
enum Node {
    Leaf { name: String, branch_length: f64 },
    Internal { branch_length: f64, children: Vec<Node> },
}
```

A type that contains itself *directly* (`left: Node`) has unbounded size and the compiler refuses. The fix: indirection through a heap pointer. [`Box<Node>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/boxed/struct.Box.html) is the most common choice. In our case, `Vec<Node>` already provides indirection â€” the children live on the heap, the enum itself is a fixed size.

## Recursive functions

```rust
fn count_tips(node: &Node) -> usize {
    match node {
        Node::Leaf { .. } => 1,
        Node::Internal { children, .. } => children.iter().map(count_tips).sum(),
    }
}
```

Rust does not guarantee tail-call optimisation, so very deep recursion can overflow the stack. For trees of biological depth this is fine; for sequences of length 10^9 it is not â€” use iteration there.

## Dynamic programming as iterative recursion

When a recursive formula visits the same subproblem many times, fill a table once instead. The recurrence becomes a nested `for` loop; each cell computed exactly once. The day-3 alignment exercise is the canonical example.

â†’ [Wikipedia: Dynamic programming](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dynamic_programming)
